summary: Gruber analyzes the strategic dynamics between Apple, Verizon, and AT&T regarding a potential Verizon iPhone, prompted by Verizon's pre-announcement of an Android tablet. He argues that Verizon's tablet announcement is likely a negotiating tactic to strengthen its position in iPhone discussions with Apple, not a genuine product commitment. He notes that Apple has maintained a skunkworks CDMA iPhone project as a contingency, and that both Apple and Verizon want each other but neither needs the other yet. The piece frames Android's rise as shifting the balance of power — giving Verizon leverage it wouldn't otherwise have against Apple.
key_points: Verizon's pre-announced Android tablet is best understood as a negotiating ploy to gain leverage over Apple in iPhone discussions, not a serious product commitment
Apple has maintained a skunkworks CDMA iPhone project as obvious contingency planning, similar to the Intel version of Mac OS X during the PowerPC era
AT&T is in the weakest position of the three parties because it needs iPhone exclusivity, while Apple and Verizon merely want each other
Android's success fundamentally changes the negotiating dynamics — without it, Verizon would need the iPhone rather than merely wanting it
Verizon's long-term interests are best served by a fragmented phone market with no dominant handset maker, even if they carry the iPhone
money_quotes: AT&T is in a weak position — they need the iPhone to remain theirs exclusively. Verizon and Apple are both in strong positions — they might want each other, but neither yet needs the other.

Imagine a hypothetical world where there was no Android, or where Android was utterly unsuccessful in the market. In that world, Apple would be in a much stronger position against Verizon — in that world, Verizon would need the iPhone.
Verizon's interests are best served in a market where there are many phone makers — Apple, RIM, HTC, Motorola, Samsung, HP — none of which are in a dominant position.
There is ZERO reason for Google and Verizon to pre-announce a tablet collaboration, likely several months before anything is available for purchase. There is only downside from something like that.
If Verizon is not soon getting the iPhone, they want a good rival. If they are (or even just may be) getting the iPhone, they want to be in the best possible negotiating position.
A strong Apple does Verizon no good, regardless of whether they're carrying the iPhone.
key_insight: The Apple-Verizon-AT&T triangle is defined by asymmetric leverage: AT&T needs the iPhone, while Apple and Verizon merely want each other, and Android's success is what keeps Verizon from needing Apple more than Apple needs them.
